title: Practical Tests and Witnesses of Fermionic non-Gaussianity
authors: Tobias Haug, Xhek Turkeshi, Piotr Sierant
abstract: Detecting when a quantum state leaves the efficiently simulable fermionic Gaussian regime is a central task for benchmarking quantum devices and certifying fermionic magic resources. We develop practical tests and witnesses based on fermionic antiflatness (FAF), a covariance-matrix-based measure of non-Gaussianity. For $n$-qubit states, we estimate FAF using two complementary protocols: two-copy Bell measurements and a single-copy scheme based on commuting matchings of Majorana bilinears. These yield testers that distinguish pure Gaussian states from states $ε$-far from the Gaussian set, using $O(n^2/ε^2)$ two-copy Bell measurements or $O(n^3/ε^4)$ single-copy measurements, improving the state of the art in the dependence on both $n$ and $ε$. For mixed states, we introduce a purity-corrected FAF witness that certifies non-Gaussianity and is highly robust to noise. With our witness, we demonstrate on the IQM quantum computer that noise can both reduce and enhance non-Gaussianity. Finally, by examining pseudo non-Gaussianity, we show that the cryptographic task of pseudorandom-state generation requires extensive fermionic non-Gaussianity. Together, these results provide experimentally accessible tools for detecting, witnessing, and quantifying non-Gaussian fermionic resources.
summary: This paper presents new, efficient protocols for detecting and quantifying non-Gaussianity in fermionic systems using both single-copy and two-copy measurement schemes. It introduces a noise-robust witness for mixed states and demonstrates the practical utility of these tools on a real quantum processor. The work establishes a fundamental link between non-Gaussianity and the complexity of generating pseudorandom fermionic states.
main_problem: Developing scalable and practical methods to detect, witness, and quantify fermionic non-Gaussianity, which is essential for benchmarking quantum devices and certifying fermionic magic resources.
main_result: The authors introduce efficient two-copy Bell measurement and single-copy protocols for estimating fermionic antiflatness (FAF), demonstrating improved scaling in qubit number and error tolerance, and showing that noise can both reduce and enhance non-Gaussianity.
method: The study utilizes two-copy Bell measurements on pairs of states and a single-copy scheme based on commuting Majorana bilinear matchings, alongside a new purity-corrected FAF witness for mixed states.
model_or_system: The system consists of n fermionic modes described by 2n Majorana operators, mapped to n qubits via the Jordan-Wigner transformation, and tested on the IQM Garnet quantum computer.
key_observables: Fermionic Antiflatness (FAF1), Gaussian fidelity (F_AF), purity-corrected FAF witness (W_FAF), and quadratic Fock-basis coherence (C2).
important_parameters: Number of qubits (n), distance to the Gaussian set (epsilon_G), noise parameter (p), and non-Gaussian gate angle (theta).
main_assumptions: The single-copy protocol assumes the ability to implement Clifford circuits and Pauli measurements; the two-copy protocol assumes access to two copies of the quantum state.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the two estimation protocols; Figure 2 details the Bell-measurement readout; Figure 3 shows experimental results on IQM Garnet; Figure 4 illustrates the evolution of the witness under various noise models; Figure 5 depicts the three-copy convolution test.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of non-Gaussianity detection, develops new two-copy and single-copy estimation protocols, provides complexity analysis, introduces a robust witness for mixed states, validates results on a quantum computer, and explores the link to pseudorandomness and gate complexity.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and quantum computing as it provides tools to certify non-trivial correlations and 'magic' resources in fermionic systems, which are harder to detect than standard entanglement or coherence.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. To test whether a given state ρ is fermionic Gaus- sian or not, we use two-copy Bell measurements or single- copy scheme based on grouping matching bilinears to estimate fermionic anti-flatness (FAF).

FIG. 3. Experimental measurement of fermionic non-Gaussianity witness WFAF on IQM Garnet quantum computer. a) State preparation circuit with n = 4 qubits of parameterized matchgates Rxx = exp(−iϕ/2X ⊗X) and Rz = exp(−iϕ/2Z) with ϕ = π/2 and non-Gaussian gate Rzz = exp(−iθ/2Z ⊗Z). b) WFAF against θ for implementation with IQM Garnet quantum computer (blue), noisy simulation (orange) and noise-free simulation (green). Noisy simulation assumes single-qubit depolarizing noise Λp = (1 −p)ρ + p I

FIG. 4. Fermionic non-Gaussianity witness WFAF in noisy matchgate (MG) circuits. a) Circuit sketch for n = 4 qubits and depth d = 3, initialized in ρ. Each layer consists of random matchgates Gij followed by local noise N. b)–d) Witness dynamics after d layers for the initial states b) |0⟩⊗n, c) the GHZ state, and d) Haar-random states. We consider local depolarizing noise, amplitude damping, dephasing, and bit-flip noise. Data are averaged over 20 random circuit instances.
